nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of Lake Malawi; found among boulders and rock piles in the upper 15 m of the water column

decorPreferences: dense rockwork with caves and overhangs
stacked limestone or tufa rock forming territory boundaries
minimal or no planting (mbuna will uproot plants)
open sandy floor for female and sub-dominant fish refuge
multiple distinct cave territories to reduce aggression

diet.notes: Primarily herbivorous; spirulina-based flake and pellets form the diet core. Supplement with blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach), algae wafers, and algae-bearing gel foods. Small amounts of frozen or live foods such as brine shrimp and daphnia are accepted but protein-heavy feeding (bloodworms, beefheart) must be strictly avoided as excess protein causes Malawi bloat, a life-threatening digestive condition in mbuna.

goodWith: other robust Lake Malawi mbuna of similar size and aggression (e.g. Pseudotropheus demasoni, Labidochromis caeruleus — yellow lab)
Plecos that can hold their own against cichlid aggression
avoidWith: Metriaclima callainos (Cobalt Blue Cichlid, fw-fish-303) — hybridizes readily in captivity; do not house together
Victorian or Tanganyikan cichlids with different water and social requirements

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo gallery for Maylandia estherae (species ID 5715); review individual image licenses before use.

imageCandidates.license: varies per image (check individual file pages)
imageCandidates.notes: Wikimedia Commons category for Maylandia estherae; multiple contributor photos showing both color morphs.

summary: A bold Lake Malawi mbuna notable for reversed sexual dichromatism: females display striking red-orange coloration while males are typically blue-grey. Highly territorial and aggressive; best kept in large, heavily rocked tanks with other robust mbuna. Never house with Cobalt Blue Cichlid (M. callainos) due to hybridization risk.
careNotes: The Red Zebra Cichlid is a classic Lake Malawi mbuna (rock-dwelling cichlid) endemic to the rocky shores of Lake Malawi. Unlike most mbuna, it exhibits reversed sexual dichromatism: females are typically red-orange or OB (orange-blotched), while males are blue-grey, sometimes with orange OB morphs also present in both sexes — making visual sexing in some color morphs unreliable without close inspection. A minimum 200 L tank (120 cm length) with extensive rockwork providing numerous caves and line-of-sight breaks is essential; the species is highly territorial and conspecific aggression is severe among males. Keep in a ratio of at least one male to three or four females to distribute male attention and prevent females from being harassed to death. Overstocking with similar-sized mbuna is a common strategy to spread aggression. Water must be hard and alkaline (pH 7.8–8.6, gH 10–20, kH 8–15) replicating Malawi’s rift lake chemistry; carbonate buffering via crushed coral substrate or limestone rock helps maintain pH stability. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are recommended. Diet must remain predominantly plant-based; protein-rich foods (especially bloodworms) trigger Malawi bloat — a rapid, often fatal digestive condition. The genus name is disputed between Maylandia and Metriaclima; both are in active use in scientific literature and the trade. The species has been extensively line-bred in captivity, producing OB (orange-blotched) and fully-orange male morphs; ensure stock source clarity to avoid unintentional hybrids. Do NOT house with Cobalt Blue Cichlid (Metriaclima callainos, fw-fish-303) as the two hybridize readily in captivity, producing hybrid offspring that are sold in the trade under both parent names.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning is triggered by good water quality and stable alkaline conditions. The male courts females with a characteristic shimmering display over a flat rock or sandy patch, and fertilization follows as the female picks up eggs in her mouth and mouths the male’s egg-spot markings on his anal fin, drawing in milt. The female carries 15–40 eggs for 21–28 days, refusing food during incubation. Separate the female to a species-only holding tank prior to expected release to protect fry from the colony; alternatively, a densely rockscaped main tank with bolt-holes can work. Fry are released at approximately 8–10 mm and accept finely crushed flake, micro-pellets, and baby brine shrimp from day one. Raise fry on a high-spirulina diet from the start to condition proper gut flora and coloration. Breedable at around 12 months; females cycle repeatedly throughout the year under aquarium conditions. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to the need to manage colony aggression and protect brooding females.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Maylandia estherae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (24–28°C), pH (7.5–8.5), hardness, native range (rocky shores of Lake Malawi), diet (herbivore, spirulina-based), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 21–28 days incubation, 15–40 eggs), conspecific aggression, and genus taxonomy note (Maylandia vs Metriaclima).

additional.name: FishBase — Maylandia estherae
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 12 cm TL), temperature (22–28°C), native range (Lake Malawi, rocky littoral), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), and diet classification (herbivore/algivore). Confirmed species ID 5715.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Mbuna Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary trade care source. Confirmed spirulina-heavy diet, Malawi bloat risk from high protein, rocky tank setup, and general mbuna husbandry parameters. No dedicated Red Zebra page; general Malawi/mbuna guide used.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Malawi Mbuna
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming territorial aggression management strategies, overstocking technique for aggression dilution, and the OB color morph and hybridization concern with M. callainos. pH 7.8–8.6 and kH buffering via limestone confirmed.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Maylandia estherae species profile
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid community source confirming reversed sexual dichromatism, hybridization with M. callainos, maternal mouthbrooding details, colony sex ratio recommendations, and minimum tank dimensions for mbuna colonies.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates 15–40 eggs for 21–28 days. Isolate brooding females to protect fry. Fry accept finely crushed spirulina flake and baby brine shrimp from release.
